journal_entry.description: Conversion of bank loan to equity, issuing 400 common shares
journal_entry.rationale: Debt-to-equity conversion: The company extinguishes a $200 liability (loan payable) by issuing 400 common shares. This is a non-cash financing transaction. Debit Loan Payable (liability decrease, slot d=1) for $200 to remove the debt from the books. Credit Common Shares (equity increase, slot b=1) for $200 to record the equity issued. No tax implications as this is a capital restructuring transaction, not a taxable supply or purchase. Total debits ($200) equal total credits ($200).

ambiguities.aspect: conversion basis and whether shares issued at par, stated value, or fair value
ambiguities.resolution: The transaction states $200 loan converted via issuing 400 shares — convention is to use book value ($200) as basis for both extinguishing the liability and crediting equity, following the stated amounts

ambiguities.aspect: whether the bank became a shareholder or loan was assigned to an existing shareholder who then converted
ambiguities.resolution: This does not affect the entry — in both scenarios, the liability is extinguished and share capital is increased by the same amounts. The identity of the shareholder is recorded in the share register, not the journal entry

ambiguities.aspect: allocation between share capital and additional paid-in capital
ambiguities.resolution: Ontario corporations under OBCA have no-par-value shares by default unless articles specify otherwise. For no-par shares, the full consideration ($200) is credited to share capital with no APIC split required

output.reason: Converting a bank loan to equity means the company is extinguishing the loan liability by issuing shares. On the debit side, the loan liability is decreased (one line). On the credit side (not classified here), common shares equity would be increased.

debit_classifier.reason: Converting a bank loan to equity means the company is extinguishing the loan liability by issuing shares. On the debit side, the loan liability is decreased (one line). On the credit side (not classified here), common shares equity would be increased.

output.reason: The transaction converts a bank loan to equity. On the credit side: (1) The loan liability of $200 is decreased (debit to Loan Payable), so this creates NO credit to liability. Wait - let me reconsider. When we decrease a liability, we DEBIT it. The credit side must show: (1) Equity increase from issuing 400 common shares for $200. This is a liability-to-equity conversion where the loan is extinguished (debited) and common shares are issued (credited). Credit side = equity increase only.

This is a liability-to-equity conversion where the loan is extinguished (debited) and common shares are issued (credited). Credit side = equity increase only.
